**If unresolved:** The Schism escalates to a Holy War Declaration eventually. The world cannot sustain two incompatible versions of the same covenant indefinitely.

---

### THE CALLING

**Trigger:** Any dynasty reaches 80% or higher faith intensity sustained for a full game cycle.

**What happens:** The covenant's influence radiates beyond the dynasty's borders. Surrounding neutral populations feel drawn toward that faith. Minor tribal communities in adjacent unclaimed territories may declare for that covenant unprompted. Populations in neighboring dynasties with low faith intensity begin reporting spiritual restlessness.

**Other dynasties' experience:** Dynasties adjacent to a dynasty experiencing The Calling receive a notification that their border populations are experiencing unusual religious interest. They may increase their own faith investment to counter it, attempt diplomacy to limit its spread, or use covert operations to disrupt the source dynasty's faith infrastructure.

**Duration:** Ongoing as long as the source dynasty maintains 80%+ intensity.

---

### THE HERESY

**Trigger:** A dynasty following Light Doctrine commits an extreme cruel action (conviction shift of -8 or more from a single action) while at moderate-to-high faith intensity.

**What happens:** A segment of the dynasty's own population recognizes the contradiction between what is professed and what is done. A Heresy Movement forms — an internal faith resistance group that believes the ruling dynasty has betrayed the covenant and must be replaced or reformed.

**Mechanical effects:** The Heresy Movement reduces faith intensity gradually from within. Covert operations from other dynasties can amplify or exploit the Heresy Movement. The dynasty must choose: suppress the movement (costly in conviction and resources, temporary solution), reform publicly (costly in capability, permanent resolution), or ignore it (the movement grows until it triggers a population revolt or territorial fracture).

**Severity:** Scales with how extreme the triggering action was and how high the faith intensity was when it occurred. A minor heresy can be resolved quickly. A major heresy at peak faith intensity is a civilizational crisis.

---

### THE HOLY WAR DECLARATION

**Trigger:** One covenant reaches 60% or more of global population share, and at least one dynasty of that covenant has reached Level 4 progression.

**What happens:** The dominant faith's most advanced Bloodline leader may declare a Holy War — a formal call to faith that transforms the conflict from dynastic competition to religious obligation. All dynasties following the declaring faith receive a morale bonus. All dynasties following opposing faiths receive a morale penalty (they know what is coming). Neutral populations begin choosing sides faster.

**Special property:** A Holy War declaration changes the diplomatic landscape fundamentally. Alliances between dynasties of different faiths are strained — populations on both sides understand that their rulers are now formally at odds with allied rulers at a level beyond politics.

**Defensive mechanic:** Other dynasties can respond to a Holy War Declaration with a Counter-Covenant — formally unifying around a competing faith to resist the dominant covenant. If two or more other covenants form a Counter-Covenant, the Holy War's morale bonus is negated and replaced with an escalating global conflict.

---

### THE COVENANT TEST

**Trigger:** Any dynasty reaches Level 4 progression in their faith development.

**What happens:** The covenant presents a Grand Test — a specific, demanding act of faith commitment that is unique to the doctrine path. The test is not optional — the covenant will not advance the dynasty to apex faith mechanics without it. The test is also not private — other dynasties know a Covenant Test is underway, though not necessarily the specifics.

**Old Light Light Doctrine Test:** Protect a population currently under siege or threat, at military cost to the protecting dynasty.

**Old Light Dark Doctrine Test:** Purge a specific faith heresy within your own population — locate and suppress a heterodox group — within a time limit.

**Blood Dominion Dark Doctrine Test:** Conduct the Great Offering — a mass ritual sacrifice within a Grand Sanctuary, consuming significant population and resources.

**Blood Dominion Light Doctrine Test:** Complete three Covenant Priest rituals in a single Level of play without combat losses during those rituals.

**The Order Light Doctrine Test:** Establish and enforce a legal code across at least three territories simultaneously — a formal governance declaration.

**The Order Dark Doctrine Test:** Subject an enemy dynasty to total institutional submission — capture their primary governance structure and hold it for a sustained period.

**The Wild Light Doctrine Test:** Establish a Grove network spanning at least three territories — connected Wild sacred sites that form a living network.

**The Wild Dark Doctrine Test:** Release the Wildfire — deliberately trigger a natural disaster or mass animal event in enemy territory without covert operations cover.

**Failure consequences:** Failed Covenant Tests reduce faith intensity significantly and lock the dynasty out of apex faith mechanics for one full Level period before they can re-attempt.

---

## Category 2 — Dynastic Events

These events trigger within the dynasty itself — within the bloodline, the family structure, the personal history.

---

### SUCCESSION CRISIS

**Trigger:** The head of a dynasty's Bloodline dies without a mature heir (under adult age threshold) or with multiple heirs of equal claim.

**What happens:** The dynasty enters a formal succession contest. Population loyalty drops in all territories as the political future is uncertain. Other dynasties may attempt marriage offers, diplomatic positioning, or military aggression during the window of instability. The succession must be resolved — by the player choosing an heir and consolidating authority — before full military and economic efficiency returns.

**Severity scale:**
- Minor: One heir, slightly young — brief instability, moderate loyalty drop
- Moderate: Two equal heirs — factional split risk within the dynasty; covert operations can amplify it
- Major: No biological heirs — requires adopting a successor, which carries legitimacy questions that persist for the rest of the match
- Catastrophic: The head dies in battle simultaneously with multiple heirs — political near-collapse requiring immediate and decisive action

**Other dynasties' options:** Marriage proposals during a succession crisis carry unusual weight — the offering dynasty gains both a diplomatic relationship and a stake in the successor's loyalty.

---

### THE CHAMPION EMERGES

**Trigger:** A non-bloodline unit survives five major engagements, each against a significantly larger force.

**What happens:** The unit's history becomes known. Their name spreads. The population knows who they are. The dynasty receives a prompt to recognize the Champion formally.

**Recognition options:**
- Grant land and title: The Champion forms a new Lesser House under the dynasty's banner. They become a permanent political entity within the dynasty's structure.
- Appoint as General: The Champion leads armies with significant battlefield bonuses. They do not become a Bloodline Member but gain equivalent battlefield presence.
- Retire with honor: Full conviction bonus (Moral). The Champion becomes a civilian figure whose reputation continues to generate population loyalty.
- Ignore: Minor conviction penalty. The Champion's legend continues without the dynasty's endorsement, which can lead to an Independent Champion event.

**Independent Champion:** If the dynasty ignores a Champion, that Champion may be recruited by another dynasty through marriage or diplomacy — creating a rival force with institutional knowledge of the original dynasty's military tactics.

---

### THE ELDER'S COUNSEL

**Trigger:** Any Bloodline Member reaches elder status (70+ years of in-game age) having survived at least three major military engagements.

**What happens:** The elder calls a formal counsel — a private meeting with the player that functions as an intelligence and strategy event. Based on the elder's lifetime of accumulated experience, the player receives one of the following (varies by the elder's specialization and life history):

- Intelligence on one opponent's military composition
- Economic assessment revealing an opponent's resource vulnerability
- A diplomatic insight — the elder's read on whether a neighboring dynasty's alliance offer is genuine
- A military recommendation — the elder identifies a tactical opening the player may not have seen

**Conviction interaction:** How the player treats the elder's counsel affects conviction. Dismissing an elder's counsel (ignoring the information entirely) is a minor cruel action. Acting on it and acknowledging the debt publicly is a minor moral action.

---

### THE PRODIGY

**Trigger:** Random rare event on any Bloodline Member birth. Probability increases with Moral conviction (parents' environment encourages exceptional development).

**What happens:** A child is born with exceptional natural aptitude in one specialization:

- Military Prodigy: Develops combat capability 50% faster than normal; reaches the maximum specialization tier earlier in their life
- Diplomatic Prodigy: Produces faith and diplomatic effects at twice the normal rate for their role
- Economic Prodigy: Generates resource bonuses in any territory they govern
- Faith Prodigy: Faith intensity increases faster in any territory where they hold a religious role
- Intellectual Prodigy: Speeds the research and development of all faith and building upgrades dynasty-wide

**Design note:** A Prodigy is not guaranteed to become a great Bloodline Member — they have exceptional potential, but that potential requires proper investment in their training and proper assignment. A Military Prodigy left in an administrative role develops administrative skills, not military ones.

---

### THE BLOOD BETRAYAL

**Trigger:** A Bloodline Member is captured and held for more than a full game cycle without ransom.

**What happens:** The captured member's continued captivity creates a wound in the dynasty. Their home territory — the one they governed or were associated with — suffers a loyalty collapse. Other Bloodline Members' morale is reduced. Recruitment in the affected territory slows.

**Resolution options:**
- Pay the ransom: The member returns. Loyalty recovers over time. A ransom paid quickly (within the first cycle) carries no conviction implications.
- Rescue by force: High-risk covert operation. If successful, major morale bonus to entire dynasty and significant conviction boost. If it fails, the captured member is executed.
- Leave them: The dynasty's population eventually accepts the loss and moves on, but the Bloodline Member's home territory's loyalty never fully recovers while the dynasty rules under the same head.
- Conduct a trade: Exchange a captured enemy Bloodline Member for your own. The cleanest resolution diplomatically, but requires having an enemy member to trade.

---

## Category 3 — Economic Events

Events triggered by resource system conditions and economic states.

---

### THE IRON DROUGHT

**Trigger:** A dynasty's iron production drops below minimum army sustenance levels for two consecutive game cycles, or a dynasty's iron territory is entirely captured.

**What happens:** Army modernization halts. No new iron-consuming units can be recruited. Existing iron-equipped units gradually lose equipment condition, reducing their combat effectiveness over time. Iron-holding territories within reach become immediately contested — other dynasties receive intelligence that someone is iron-poor.

**Recovery:** Retake iron territory, establish a trade route for iron with a willing ally, or disband iron-consuming units to reduce iron demand (Born of Sacrifice cycle — the recycled troops are a resource response, not just a tactical one).

**Strategic note:** The Iron Drought is often more valuable as a threat than as an actual event — the credible ability to deny iron to an enemy can be more powerful than the denial itself.

---

### THE CURRENCY CONTEST

**Trigger:** Two dynasties are both pursuing the Economic Victory path and both reach 40% or higher currency adoption.

**What happens:** The world economy cannot sustain two competing currency standards at high adoption simultaneously. A formal Currency Contest is declared — a time-limited period during which both dynasties actively compete for the final global adoption threshold. If one dynasty reaches 60% within the contest window, the other is forced to retreat to gold standard.

**Military escalation mechanic:** If the Currency Contest is unresolved at the end of the window, it triggers a Currency Resolution War — a formal military conflict where the outcome determines economic dominance. Other dynasties may side with either competitor or attempt to exploit the conflict to advance their own position.

---

### THE ABUNDANT HARVEST

**Trigger:** A dynasty's food production exceeds population needs by 50% or more for three consecutive game cycles.

**What happens:** A genuine food surplus generates. Population growth accelerates beyond its normal rate. A recruitment window opens — extra population is available for military service at reduced conscription cost during the window. Trade partners offer better terms for food.

**Optional action:** The dynasty may choose to share the surplus with a neighboring dynasty or with the Trueborn City. Sharing with a neighbor is a diplomatic gesture with relationship consequences. Sharing with the Trueborn City generates significant moral conviction and Trueborn trust.

---

### THE GREAT FAMINE

**Trigger:** Food production falls below population minimum for two consecutive game cycles.

**What happens:** Population decline begins. Faith intensity drops as spiritual engagement falls to survival priority. Conviction shifts slightly toward Cruel if no player action is taken (the population perceives inaction as indifference). Military morale drops across all units.

**Response options:**
- Emergency rationing: Reduces population decline rate but imposes happiness penalty
- Emergency recruitment freeze: No new soldiers; all available food prioritized for civilians
- Trade for food: Diplomatic option requiring a willing partner and gold
- Allow decline: Conviction cost; eventual population stabilization at lower level
- Take food by force from a neighbor: Major conviction cost, starts a war, solves the food problem

**Conviction interaction:** How the dynasty responds to famine is one of the most visible conviction moments in the game. The population is watching.

---

### THE MARKET COLLAPSE

**Trigger:** A dynasty's gold income drops below operating costs for one full game cycle while having outstanding diplomatic commitments that require gold payment.

**What happens:** The dynasty defaults on its diplomatic commitments. Alliance agreements become strained or broken. Mercenary contracts (if active) are void. The dynasty's reputation for financial reliability is damaged — future trade negotiations are less favorable.

**Recovery:** Restore gold production, sell assets through the Trueborn City market, or renegotiate diplomatic commitments through Whitehall mediators.

---

## Category 4 — Military Events

Events triggered by battlefield conditions and army states.

---

### THE ROUT

**Trigger:** An army is destroyed with 90% or higher casualties in a single engagement.

**What happens:** The survivors, if any, carry a Rout status. Their morale is severely reduced and recovers at half the normal rate. More significantly, the news of the rout spreads — enemy dynasties learn of the defeat, and the losing dynasty's military reputation takes a temporary penalty affecting recruitment willingness.

**Recovery:** A Bloodline Member must formally address the survivors and the population — a gameplay event where the player chooses the framing (inspire with vengeance, honor the fallen, pledge to do better). The choice has minor conviction implications and determines how quickly morale recovers.

---

### THE STAND

**Trigger:** An army holding a defensive position successfully repels an attacking force with at least twice its numbers.

**What happens:** The defending units immediately gain Veteran status (if they don't already have it). The dynasty's military reputation receives a significant positive signal — populations in adjacent territories take notice. Conviction shifts slightly toward Moral (defense against overwhelming odds resonates morally with the population).

**Named event:** The battle is named. The player may name it, or the game assigns a name based on the location. Named battles accumulate in the dynasty's history log and become part of the Prestige Dynastic Victory calculation.

---

### THE DESERTION WAVE

**Trigger:** Dynasty conviction crosses into Cruel threshold while an army is on extended campaign (away from home territory for two or more game cycles).

**What happens:** A percentage of active troops desert during the night phase. The percentage scales with how deeply into Cruel conviction the dynasty is. Troops closer to home territory desert at lower rates than troops on extended campaign. Units led by Bloodline Members desert at lower rates than units with appointed generals.

**Prevention:** Bloodline Member presence, morale investment, shorter campaigns, regular rotation of troops back to home territory.

**Escalation:** A single Desertion Wave is survivable. Repeated Desertion Waves strip armies bare. A dynasty that reaches extreme Cruel conviction and maintains extended campaigns simultaneously is militarily destabilizing itself.

---

### THE SIEGE BREAKS

**Trigger:** A dynasty successfully defends a territory against a siege that lasted three or more game cycles.

**What happens:** The defending dynasty's population in that territory gains a permanent loyalty bonus — they held, and holding means something. The territory's faith intensity increases (hardship deepens faith). The defending units gain accelerated experience from the sustained defense.

**Other dynasties' awareness:** The successful defense is known. The besieging dynasty's reputation for siege capability takes a temporary penalty. Dynasties considering allying with the besieging house reconsider the alliance value.

---

### THE CHAMPION'S DUEL

**Trigger:** Two Bloodline Members from opposing dynasties are both present on the same battlefield.

**What happens:** The game offers both players the option to call a formal Champion's Duel — a one-on-one engagement between the two Bloodline Members that determines the battle outcome without full engagement. Both players must agree. If only one agrees, the duel does not occur (but the agreement offer is visible and refusing a duel is a minor reputation event).

**Duel outcome:** Determined by the Bloodline Members' accumulated experience, specialization, and current health. A Duel is not purely randomized — a Military-specialized Bloodline Member at full health against a Faith-specialized Bloodline Member will win with high probability. But upsets are possible.

**Duel stakes:** The losing Bloodline Member is captured (not killed, unless the winner's dynasty has specific conviction and faith mechanics that mandate execution). The winning side's army receives a massive morale bonus for the remainder of the engagement.

---

## Category 5 — Diplomatic Events

Events triggered by the relationships between dynasties.

---

### THE MARRIAGE PROPOSAL

**Trigger:** Any dynasty reaches Level 2 progression and has a marriageable Bloodline Member.

**What happens:** Other dynasties may send formal marriage proposals. The player receives the proposal with available information about the proposing dynasty and the proposed arrangement.

**Marriage outcomes:**
- Full alliance: Both dynasties share intelligence and cannot declare war on each other without a formal Betrayal (major conviction cost)
- Trade alliance: Favorable trade terms exchanged; military independence maintained
- Political arrangement: Symbolic alliance with no mechanical binding
- Rejection: No mechanical penalty if declined politely; diplomatic damage if declined insultingly

**Cross-faith marriages:** A marriage between dynasties of different faiths creates a mixed household. The child of such a marriage may follow either parent's faith, have an unusual relationship with both, or in rare cases develop a personal faith expression that does not fit either parent's covenant neatly.

---

### THE TRUEBORN SUMMONS

**Trigger:** Any dynasty approaches military hegemony — holding more than 55% of total map territory or having eliminated three or more other dynasties.

**What happens:** The Trueborn City sends a formal summons to all remaining dynasties. The Trueborn Coalition window opens. Dynasties may join the coalition voluntarily. The hegemon receives a formal notification that a coalition is forming against them.

**Coalition mechanics:** Dynasties that join the coalition share military intelligence and receive a small morale bonus against the hegemon specifically. The coalition does not merge the dynasties — they remain independent actors with their own victory conditions. The coalition is focused specifically on reducing the hegemon's territory below the hegemony threshold.

**Hegemon response options:**
- Accept limitation: Voluntarily reduce military presence in contested territories; the coalition dissolves
- Negotiate with individual coalition members: Break the coalition through diplomacy, trade, or marriage
- Fight the coalition: Win decisively or face eventual attrition against multiple opponents
- Accelerate victory: Push harder and faster before the coalition can fully coordinate

---

### THE ALLIANCE BETRAYAL

**Trigger:** A dynasty declares war on a dynasty it has a formal alliance with.

**What happens:** Major conviction penalty (Cruel shift). The betraying dynasty's diplomatic relationships with every other dynasty are damaged — they have demonstrated they will break formal agreements when convenient. The betrayed dynasty's population receives a significant loyalty boost (outrage at the betrayal unifies them). The Trueborn City records the betrayal and the betraying dynasty's trust with the City drops significantly.

**Mechanical note:** This event exists to make the formal alliance meaningful. Without a cost to betrayal, alliances are just words. With a significant cost, alliances become genuine commitments.

---

### THE NEUTRAL ARBITRATION

**Trigger:** Two dynasties are in sustained conflict (three or more game cycles of continuous war) and at least one of them has built up sufficient Trueborn City trust.

**What happens:** The Trueborn City offers to arbitrate. Both dynasties may agree or decline. If both agree, the arbitration produces a formal peace framework with specific terms (territory, resources, prisoner exchange). Both dynasties receive a conviction bonus for accepting arbitration (the willingness to pursue peace through legitimate means is morally coded).

**If one declines:** The declining dynasty loses Trueborn trust. The accepting dynasty gains trust. This asymmetry creates pressure toward arbitration even when the military situation might favor continuing the conflict.

---

## Category 6 — World Events

Events that affect the entire game world, triggered by macro-level conditions.

---

### THE FROST ECHO

**Trigger:** Late game (Level 4 or higher), any dynasty occupies Tundra territory during a winter cycle with low overall world development index.

**What happens:** A miniature climate event. Frost conditions temporarily extend south from Tundra into adjacent Badlands and occasionally into Stone Highlands territories. This is not the Great Frost returning — it is a weather echo, lasting one game cycle. During the Frost Echo:

- Frost Ruin territories in the affected zone become accessible with unique resource caches (preserved goods surviving from the Age Before, not yet thawed)
- Armies in the affected zone take attrition damage
- Population in affected territories loses happiness
- The Wild faith units are unaffected by the Echo attrition

**World event visibility:** All dynasties are aware a Frost Echo is occurring. It is a shared experience. The Trueborn City makes a formal statement about it.

---

### THE OLD LIGHT RETURNS

**Trigger:** A Trueborn dynasty emerges (if Trueborn is a player-controlled dynasty that has reached Level 3 and publicly revealed their identity claim).

**What happens:** A signal event. All faith intensities in the world rise slightly — the Trueborn re-emergence resonates with every covenant's ancient memory. The Trueborn City's council convenes a special session. Other dynasties must formally respond: acknowledge the claim, challenge it, or remain silent. Silence has diplomatic implications. Acknowledgment changes the political landscape significantly.

**Mechanical effects:** If the claim is broadly acknowledged, the Trueborn dynasty gains significant faith intensity in all territories and a global diplomatic aura that makes war against them politically costly for any who declared acknowledgment.

---

### THE GREAT RECKONING

**Trigger:** One dynasty holds 70% or more of total map territory.

**What happens:** All remaining dynasties receive a global coalition bonus against the hegemon specifically — morale, recruitment capacity, and faith intensity are boosted for dynasties in formal opposition. The Trueborn City mobilizes its neutral military forces for the first time in the match — not as an army that attacks, but as a defensive guarantor for any territory that requests Trueborn City protection.

**This event marks the final phase of the match:** Either the hegemon completes the conquest before the coalition can coordinate, or the coalition reduces the hegemon's territory below the trigger threshold and the match continues.

---

### THE FAITH CONVERGENCE

**Trigger:** Three or more dynasties follow the same covenant at high intensity simultaneously.

**What happens:** The faith in question gains a world presence that exceeds what any individual dynasty represents. The faith begins operating partially independently — populations in neutral territories convert without any dynasty's direct involvement. Sacred Ground affiliated with the convergent faith begins generating higher intensity spontaneously.

**Competitive reaction:** Other faiths experience this as existential pressure. Dynasties following minority faiths receive a "Defender of the Faith" bonus — increased faith intensity and morale — as their populations recognize they are in the minority and commit more deeply.

**Endgame implication:** A Faith Convergence can accelerate a Faith Victory path or can trigger the most significant Holy War the match has seen.

---

======================================================================
# PART H — UNIT INDEX
======================================================================

# Bloodlines — Unit Index

Registry of all unit types, army composition rules, and elite unit specifications.

### Content Added 2026-03-15

## Standard Units — Level 1 (Earliest Military Stage)

Level 1 represents the earliest military stage. Armies are small and drawn from the population.

### Militia
Basic soldiers drawn directly from the population. Very cheap, weak combat ability, low morale. Used for emergency defense and early expansion.

### Swordsmen
The first organized professional soldiers. Moderate attack and defense. Faster movement speed. Increased line of sight. Serve as both frontline infantry and reconnaissance units.

**Design Decision:** Scouts are intentionally removed as a unit type. Swordsmen replace the scouting role. This was an explicit design change made during Session 2.

### Spearmen
Defensive infantry with higher defense than swordsmen. Slower movement. Effective at holding the line. Strong against cavalry.

### Hunters
Light ranged units drawn from the population. Used for harassment and ambush. Weak in melee combat.

### Bowmen
Upgraded ranged units trained from hunters. Bowmen can specialize in two roles:
- **Offensive Bowmen** — Higher attack damage
- **Defensive Bowmen** — Increased range and improved defensive accuracy

Bowmen remain weak in close combat.

## Standard Units — Levels 2-5

---

### Level 2 Units — The Iron Transition (Added 2026-03-18, Fifth Session)

Level 2 represents the shift from survival to consolidation. Iron begins flowing from mines into the armory. The army gains specialization for the first time. Cavalry appears on the field.

**Design principle:** Level 2 units are still available to all dynasties regardless of faith. Faith has been chosen by the end of Level 1 but has not yet reshaped the military. These units represent the first age of real professional military organization.

---

#### Men-at-Arms
Full iron armor on the body of a Swordsman. Slower movement, dramatically more durable. The backbone of mid-game army formations. Where Swordsmen are versatile and mobile, Men-at-Arms are immovable anchors.

**Role:** Heavy frontline infantry
**Strength:** Exceptional durability under sustained attack; morale anchor for adjacent units
**Weakness:** Slower movement reduces ability to pursue or retreat; iron cost is ongoing
**Countered by:** Crossbowmen (armor-piercing), flanking maneuvers, siege weapons
**Requirement:** Armory (upgraded), iron supply

---

#### Pike Square
An upgraded formation of Spearmen reorganized into a disciplined pike formation. Devastating against cavalry charges. Largely immobile — they hold a position, not advance on one.

**Role:** Anti-cavalry anchor, defensive line
**Strength:** Negates cavalry charges completely; high defense when stationary; forces enemies to route around
**Weakness:** Immobile in formation; vulnerable to ranged fire; useless when flanked without adjacent infantry support
**Countered by:** Crossbowmen, flanking infantry, siege weapons
**Requirement:** Training Grounds (upgraded)
**Formation bonus:** Two or more adjacent Pike Square units form a continuous wall with escalating defense bonus — each additional unit in the wall increases the wall's resistance

---

#### Scout Rider
The first cavalry unit. Light, fast, built for harassment and intelligence gathering rather than sustained combat. Dies quickly against formed infantry. Invaluable for disrupting supply lines and forcing repositioning.

**Role:** Light cavalry, raid and reconnaissance
**Strength:** Exceptional speed; destroys undefended resource nodes and Lumber Camps; forces enemies to respond to threats rather than executing their plan
**Weakness:** Paper-thin against formed infantry; countered by Pike Squares immediately
**Countered by:** Any formed infantry unit; Pike Square especially
**Requirement:** Stable (horses), iron (horseshoes and equipment)
**Special:** Can capture undefended resource nodes without siege, forcing the enemy to divert forces

---

#### Crossbowmen
The most important ranged upgrade of the mid-game. Slower rate of fire than Bowmen but dramatically more effective against armored targets. Changes how siege and battle formations work.

**Role:** Armor-piercing ranged suppression
**Strength:** Pierces Men-at-Arms armor; effective against fortified positions from medium range; steady-state suppression during sieges
**Weakness:** Slow reload cycle; weak in melee; needs infantry screen
**Countered by:** Fast cavalry closing quickly; flanking; shield wall infantry
**Requirement:** Armory, Training Grounds
**Note:** The introduction of Crossbowmen is the primary reason Men-at-Arms are not unbeatable — the design creates an ongoing arms race between armor and armor-piercing capability

---

#### Siege Engineer
Not a combat unit. Tears down walls, builds field fortifications, and enables siege operations. A force with Siege Engineers can take fortified positions. A force without them cannot.

**Role:** Siege and field construction
**Strength:** Only unit capable of actively dismantling walls and gates; can construct field barricades and rudimentary fortifications
**Weakness:** Completely defenseless; must be screened by combat units; slow movement
**Countered by:** Any combat unit if unescorted
**Requirement:** Armory + Training Grounds
**Special:** Can sabotage enemy resource infrastructure (slower than raiding cavalry, but more thorough — disables rather than just damages)

---

### Level 3 Units — Ideological Expansion Era (Added 2026-03-18, Fifth Session)

Level 3 is where faith begins reshaping the military. All dynasties retain access to base unit types, but faith investment unlocks specialized forces that are qualitatively different from standard military units. Iron demand peaks at Level 3. The armies that emerge at this level will carry civilizational identity visibly.

---

#### Base Units Available to All Dynasties

---

##### Knight
The defining cavalry unit of the mid-game. Full iron barding on horse and rider. The terror of open-field engagements against unformed infantry. Expensive in iron, expensive in population, and countered hard by terrain and pike formations — but worth it when conditions align.

**Role:** Heavy cavalry, shock weapon
**Strength:** Open-field devastation against infantry not in pike formation; extreme morale impact on enemy units struck in the charge; pursuit capability against routing enemies
**Weakness:** Completely negated by Pike Square formation; poor in forest and rough terrain; iron costs are substantial and ongoing
**Countered by:** Pike Squares, rough terrain, Crossbowmen sustained fire
**Requirement:** Stable (warhorses), Armory, iron (heavy)
**Special mechanic — The Charge:** A Knight unit that has not engaged in the current engagement gains a charge bonus on first contact. After the charge is spent, they fight as heavy cavalry without the bonus until they disengage and reform.

---

##### Longbowmen
Range and accuracy that changes siege dynamics. A trained Longbowman unit at elevation can suppress fortified positions and break up assault formations before they reach the walls. Among the most tactically versatile ranged units in the game.

**Role:** Elite ranged suppression, siege fire support
**Strength:** Greater range than Crossbowmen; effective at elevation; sustained fire during sieges; can break charge formations before impact
**Weakness:** Weather-sensitive (strong wind penalties); requires space to operate effectively; weak in melee
**Countered by:** Cavalry closing fast; melee infantry overwhelming their screen
**Requirement:** Training Grounds (upgraded), Archery Range (new Military building, Level 3)
**Unlock note:** Longbowmen replace Bowmen as the primary ranged unit at Level 3. Bowmen may still be recruited at lower cost if needed.

---

##### Shield Wall
A unit built entirely around defense. A Shield Wall unit that holds a position and is not flanked is among the most durable infantry formations in the game. It does not advance. It absorbs.

**Role:** Defensive anchor, morale anchor
**Strength:** Exceptional damage absorption from frontal assault; morale aura — adjacent units do not rout as long as the Shield Wall holds; can hold a position indefinitely against infantry assault
**Weakness:** No offensive capability; loses all effectiveness if flanked; cannot pursue; will eventually fall to sustained Crossbow or Longbow fire
**Countered by:** Flanking maneuvers, ranged sustained fire
**Requirement:** Armory, Barracks (upgraded)
**Formation bonus:** Two or more Shield Wall units adjacent create a wall that provides a morale anchor to the entire formation behind them — armies fighting from behind a Shield Wall have higher baseline morale throughout the engagement

---

##### Siege Ballista
Heavy siege weapon. Fixed position, cannot move while deployed. Tears down walls efficiently. Effective against densely packed infantry. Has a long deployment time that must be protected during setup.

**Role:** Siege, anti-formation weapon
**Strength:** Most efficient wall and gate destruction of Level 3; high single-hit damage against infantry clusters; psychological effect on defenders watching a ballista aim at their position
**Weakness:** Fixed when deployed; long setup time; requires engineer crew (tied to Siege Engineer unit or specialist crew); vulnerable during setup
**Countered by:** Cavalry charges during setup; Longbowmen fire during setup; sally-out infantry assault
**Requirement:** Engineering Camp (new Military building, Level 3), wood + iron

---

#### Faith-Influenced Level 3 Units

These units require Temple + Shrine investment in addition to military prerequisites. They are not superior in every context — they are specialized, and their power is situational and faith-consistent.

---

##### Old Light — Flame Warden (Light Doctrine)
Support infantry that carry consecrated fire in controlled forms — lanterns, braziers, sacred torches. Their presence restores morale to adjacent units after battle. They are not primarily fighters — they are sustainers.

**Role:** Post-combat morale restoration, sustained campaign support
**Mechanics:** Units adjacent to a Flame Warden begin recovering morale faster after each engagement. Armies with Flame Wardens can sustain longer campaigns without returning to settlement for morale recovery.
**Doctrine requirement:** Old Light, Light doctrine active, Temple built
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### Old Light — Inquisitor (Dark Doctrine)
The Old Light's dark doctrine produces this unit: disciplined suppressors of competing faith expressions. Inquisitors do not fight armies — they fight ideological environments. In conquered territory, they disrupt enemy faith intensity. In their own territory, they identify and suppress heterodox belief.

**Role:** Faith suppression, psychological warfare
**Mechanics:** Deployed in captured territory, Inquisitors gradually reduce the enemy faith intensity of the local population. This is slow and methodical — not instant. Enemy dynasties will notice their faith intensity dropping in occupied regions and must choose to retake the territory or accept the ideological erosion.
**Secondary mechanic:** Inquisitors also detect covert operations more effectively — their training in identifying hidden beliefs translates to identifying hidden agents.
**Doctrine requirement:** Old Light, Dark doctrine active, Temple built
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### Blood Dominion — Blood Rider (Dark Doctrine)
Cavalry that grows stronger as allied units die nearby. They feed on the sacrifice of their comrades — mechanically, not just thematically. A Blood Rider unit engaged in a battle where friendly casualties are occurring gains escalating attack power. They are terrifying in attritional battles where losses are ongoing.

**Role:** Escalating damage cavalry, morale disruptor
**Mechanics:** Each friendly unit destroyed within range of a Blood Rider unit provides a stacking attack bonus for the duration of the engagement. This bonus resets between engagements.
**Design tension:** Blood Riders are strongest when the army is losing — this creates a gameplay decision about whether to sacrifice weakening units deliberately to power Blood Riders, at the cost of those units and their population.
**Doctrine requirement:** Blood Dominion, Dark doctrine active, Temple built
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### Blood Dominion — Covenant Priest (Light Doctrine)
A ritualist who performs animal sacrifice during or immediately before battle to invoke a short-term surge in morale and attack. Not a combatant. Must be protected. But a Covenant Priest who successfully completes a pre-battle ritual can change the outcome of an engagement that would otherwise be lost.

**Role:** Pre-battle ritual support, morale spike
**Mechanics:** At the beginning of an engagement, the Covenant Priest may perform a ritual (consuming one unit of livestock from the Ranch) to provide a morale and attack bonus to all adjacent allied units for a short duration. The ritual takes time — time that the enemy may disrupt with ranged fire or a fast advance.
**Doctrine requirement:** Blood Dominion, Light doctrine active, Temple built
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### The Order — Iron Legionary (Light Doctrine)
Heavy infantry with disciplined shield formation capability. The Iron Legionary can form a testudo — an interlocking shield formation that is nearly impenetrable from the front and highly resistant from the sides. In this formation, the unit cannot attack. It moves slowly. But it cannot be broken by anything short of siege weapons or extreme flanking.

**Role:** Near-impenetrable advance formation, breakthrough unit
**Mechanics:** Two modes — standard combat mode and testudo formation. In testudo, the unit has dramatically increased defense in all directions and is immune to ranged fire. Movement speed is reduced by two-thirds. Cannot attack in testudo. Transitioning between modes takes several seconds.
**Design use:** Iron Legionaries in testudo can advance through ranged fire that would destroy any other unit, reach a fortified position, and then transition back to combat mode for the assault.
**Doctrine requirement:** The Order, Light doctrine active, Temple built
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### The Order — Warden-Enforcer (Dark Doctrine)
Military police with a dark doctrine mandate. Warden-Enforcers suppress desertion and panic within their own army and inflict fear penalties on enemy routing units — preventing routing enemies from rallying. Their presence makes a defeated army more defeated and their own army more obedient.

**Role:** Internal discipline, rout suppression
**Mechanics:** Allied units within range of a Warden-Enforcer cannot rout regardless of morale — they hold position at all costs, including death. Enemy routing units near a Warden-Enforcer take additional morale damage and are less likely to rally. Warden-Enforcers can also execute deserters, producing a minor resource return and a morale impact on remaining troops (positive for discipline, negative for morale — a tradeoff).
**Doctrine requirement:** The Order, Dark doctrine active, Temple built
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### The Wild — Thornrider (Light Doctrine)
Light cavalry on elk or large stags rather than horses. Dramatically faster than horse cavalry in forested and rough terrain. Virtually invisible in dense forest while stationary. A Thornrider unit that attacks from forest concealment has a significant first-strike advantage.

**Role:** Forest cavalry, ambush strike
**Strength:** Fast in forests; invisible when stationary in forest; significant first-strike bonus from concealment
**Weakness:** Poor armor; poor on open plains against formed cavalry; cannot pursue into non-forest terrain efficiently
**Countered by:** Watchtower networks that reveal forest movement; open-terrain cavalry
**Doctrine requirement:** The Wild, Light doctrine active, Temple built, forest territory nearby
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate

---

##### The Wild — Bear Rider (Dark Doctrine)
Heavy animal cavalry on bears. Not fast — bears are not horses — but the intimidation mechanic is real and mechanically expressed. Enemy units with low morale will break before the Bear Riders engage them. Enemy units with moderate morale must make a morale check on contact. Only high-morale units resist.

**Role:** Intimidation cavalry, morale crusher
**Strength:** Intimidation aura that affects low-morale enemies before combat begins; significantly more durable than horse cavalry in close terrain; effective against routing enemies
**Weakness:** Slower than horse cavalry; countered by high-morale units with pike formations; bears require specialized feeding (consumes food at higher rate than horse cavalry)
**Special:** The Wild's dark doctrine mechanic — at high faith intensity, Bear Riders can be enhanced with ritual preparation that increases their intimidation aura range and duration
**Doctrine requirement:** The Wild, Dark doctrine active, Temple built, animal husbandry buildings
**Faith intensity threshold:** Moderate-high

---

### Level 4 Units — Irreversible Divergence (Added 2026-03-18, Fifth Session)

Level 4 is the point of no return. Every unit at this level is faith-specific. A dynasty that has not committed to a faith with serious investment cannot field these units. They are rare, expensive, individually powerful. Losing one is felt in the army's morale. They carry names and weight.

These units should be thought of as sub-hero units — not as numerous as standard forces, never fielded in large numbers, but capable of changing the course of an engagement through their presence.

---

##### Old Light — Sunblade Champion (Light Doctrine)
The apex expression of Old Light light doctrine military power. An individually consecrated warrior who heals on kill, restores adjacent unit morale, and radiates a passive inspiration to nearby troops. Not the most damaging unit — the most sustaining.

**Role:** Battlefield sustainer, morale anchor, elite fighter
**Mechanics:** On each kill, restores a small portion of morale to adjacent allied units. Passive aura reduces morale loss for all units within a moderate radius. Can perform a "Lay on Hands" ability once per engagement that fully restores morale of one adjacent unit.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes
**Conviction interaction:** Moral conviction amplifies the healing and morale effects. A Cruel-conviction dynasty fielding a Sunblade Champion receives reduced effects as the population perceives the contradiction.

---

##### Old Light — Purge Templar (Dark Doctrine)
Inquisitor cavalry. Elite riders who target enemy bloodline members and faith practitioners specifically. Dramatically effective against enemy commanders with religious roles. Their presence forces an enemy to protect their faith leadership.

**Role:** Counter-commander cavalry, faith practitioner hunter
**Mechanics:** Bonus damage against enemy Bloodline Members, faith units, and enemy commanders. Can identify and target faith-aligned enemies in battle. When a Purge Templar kills an enemy Bloodline Member, surrounding enemy units suffer a severe morale penalty.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes
**Conviction interaction:** Dark conviction amplifies their ruthlessness. A dynasty with Moral conviction suffers a recruitment cost penalty for Purge Templars — the population does not produce these willingly.

---

##### Blood Dominion — Bloodbound (Dark Doctrine)
A unit that cannot retreat. A unit that fights to the death. As it takes wounds, it grows stronger in attack. As it takes casualties within its formation, the survivors become more dangerous. It is the most frightening unit to face in a narrow engagement.

**Role:** Suicidal assault, last-resort siege breaker
**Mechanics:** Cannot receive a retreat order. Cannot rout. As the unit's HP drops, attack power increases proportionally — a Bloodbound at 25% health hits harder than it did at full health. When other Blood Dominion units rout nearby, Bloodbound absorbs their conviction and gains a temporary surge.
**Design note:** The Bloodbound cannot be saved once committed. This is intentional. The player chooses to send them knowing they will not return. The question is whether the damage they inflict justifies the population cost.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes
**Conviction interaction:** Cruel conviction is essentially required — a Moral conviction dynasty cannot produce Bloodbound recruits because the population will not volunteer for suicide missions.

---

##### Blood Dominion — Covenant Scion (Light Doctrine)
A consecrated warrior assigned permanently to a specific Bloodline Member as their guardian. Not a free unit — they are assigned to a person and fight harder as that person's survival is threatened.

**Role:** Bloodline Member bodyguard, escalating defender
**Mechanics:** Assigned to one Bloodline Member. Provides a defensive bonus to that member at all times. As the protected member's health drops, the Scion's attack and defense increase proportionally. A Covenant Scion fighting to protect a wounded Bloodline Member is among the most dangerous individual combatants in the game.
**Special:** If the assigned Bloodline Member is killed, the Covenant Scion does not die — they enter a state called Blood Grief, gaining maximum possible attack for a limited duration before their morale collapses.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes

---

##### The Order — Iron Sentinel (Light Doctrine)
Tower-equivalent infantry. They plant themselves in one location and hold it indefinitely. They do not advance, do not pursue, do not respond to orders that would move them from their position. They simply hold. In the right position, an Iron Sentinel unit can change the tactical geometry of an entire battlefield.

**Role:** Fixed defensive position, chokepoint holder
**Mechanics:** When the "Root" command is given, the Iron Sentinel becomes stationary and gains dramatically increased defense in all directions. They hold this position until destroyed or ordered to disengage. In Root state, they cannot be moved by morale damage — they hold regardless of what happens around them. One Iron Sentinel unit in a narrow pass renders that pass impassable without siege weapons.
**Weakness:** Once the enemy understands their position, they route around rather than through. Effective only in terrain that channels enemies through the Sentinel's position.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes

---

##### The Order — Dominion Arbiter (Dark Doctrine)
Eliminates the morale mechanic for routed enemies. When enemy units rout, they typically recover morale over time and may rally. The Dominion Arbiter pursues routing enemies and executes them before they can rally. They stop the cycle of rout-and-recover that makes extended battles possible.

**Role:** Rout suppression, pursuit execution
**Mechanics:** Automatically pursues routing enemy units and eliminates them before rally can occur. Does not engage in standard battle — only pursues routing targets. Exceptionally fast. Cannot be stopped from pursuit without losing the target.
**Design effect:** The Dominion Arbiter changes the risk calculus of battle. Armies facing an Order dynasty with Arbiter units cannot afford to let their line bend — any unit that routes will be eliminated, not just temporarily weakened.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes

---

##### The Wild — Forest Sovereign (Light Doctrine)
A Bloodline Member equivalent unit of the faith. The Forest Sovereign does not fight personally — they call a territorial aura in forested terrain that transforms every Wild unit in range into something more than they were. This unit is among the strongest force multipliers in the game in the right terrain.

**Role:** Faith force multiplier, terrain-locked aura unit
**Mechanics:** Creates a territorial aura in forested terrain within range. All Wild units in the aura gain: increased speed, forest stealth, and enhanced attack. The aura moves with the Forest Sovereign. Loses all power outside of forested terrain — in open ground, this unit has no special mechanics.
**Weakness:** Completely terrain-dependent. An enemy who forces the Wild army to fight in open terrain neutralizes this unit.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes

---

##### The Wild — Bonebreaker (Dark Doctrine)
A Bear Rider evolved into something that primarily attacks structures rather than infantry. A single Bonebreaker can destroy a Lumber Camp in a charge, disable a Quarry, and demolish a Stable. It is the Wild's answer to siege weapons — not a weapon of war but of economic violence.

**Role:** Structure destruction, economic sabotage
**Mechanics:** Optimized for structure damage. Against infantry, average. Against resource buildings, devastating — four to five times faster than a Siege Ballista at destroying undefended economic infrastructure. Can also target Watchtowers and Shrines.
**Special — Born of Sacrifice eligible:** A Bonebreaker that has destroyed three or more structures during its service life becomes eligible for a Born of Sacrifice cycle that produces an extraordinary successor.
**Grand Sanctuary requirement:** Yes

---

### Level 5 Units — Apex Forces (Added 2026-03-18, Fifth Session)

One unit type per faith at the apex. These exist in small numbers — one or two per dynasty, maximum. They are the legacy of a civilization's total commitment to its covenant. They carry names. They are remembered after the match.

---

#### The Unbroken — Old Light Apex
A consecrated battalion that cannot be broken by morale damage. Only death removes them from the field. They fight with the morale bonus of a dynasty at full Moral conviction regardless of circumstances. Even in a losing battle, The Unbroken hold. This is not AI behavior — it is a mechanical state: morale damage does not apply to them.

**Population cost:** Very high — only a dynasty with large, loyal population can field them
**Faith cost:** Requires Grand Sanctuary and sustained maximum faith intensity for one full Level progression
**Conviction requirement:** Moral conviction only — Cruel conviction dynasties cannot produce The Unbroken
**Battlefield effect:** Any allied unit adjacent to The Unbroken is immune to rout for the duration of the engagement

---

#### The Sacrificed — Blood Dominion Apex
The ultimate expression of the Born of Sacrifice system applied to the faith. A unit created through the actual sacrifice of a Bloodline Member — named for that member, carrying their spirit (mechanically: inheriting all accumulated experience of that member). The most individually powerful unit in the game.

**Creation:** Requires Grand Sanctuary, maximum faith intensity (dark or light), and the deliberate sacrifice of one Bloodline Member. The sacrifice is a player choice, not a mechanic that occurs automatically.
**Power:** The Sacrificed unit retains the combat experience and specialization of the sacrificed Bloodline Member in addition to its own apex combat capabilities.
**Identity:** Named for the sacrificed member. The name persists through the match. If The Sacrificed is destroyed, the dynasty records the loss of both the unit and the original member.
**Conviction effect:** Creating The Sacrificed shifts conviction significantly toward Cruel — the population understands what happened, even if they do not witness it directly. There is no moral-conviction path to The Sacrificed.

---

#### The Mandate — The Order Apex
A perfectly drilled, disciplined force with no morale system. They feel no fear, no hesitation, no retreat. They execute orders and die in formation. The Mandate does not rout. The Mandate does not panic. The Mandate does not disobey.

**Mechanical property:** Complete morale immunity — not "resistant," not "immune to rout," but genuinely morale-free. No morale damage, no morale bonus. They are at the same functional state at the beginning and the end of a losing battle.
**Special:** The Mandate can be ordered to do things no other unit can be ordered to do — hold a position against impossible odds, advance into certain casualties, defend a building that will certainly fall. Other units would break. The Mandate executes.
**Creation requirement:** Grand Sanctuary, maximum Order faith intensity, full Level 5 progression
**Limitation:** If every member of the formation is killed, the formation is gone. They do not retreat to rebuild. There is no survivors contingency. The Mandate is a single-use weapon of certainty.

---

#### The First Wild — The Wild Apex
Not quite an army. A force that brings the Age Before with it when it walks. The First Wild is a unit formation built around a core of apex Wild warriors accompanied by a pack — bears, wolves, stags — that responds to the formation's faith aura rather than direct control. When The First Wild approaches, the terrain responds. Forests deepen around them. Water rises in streams. Animals in the region move toward them.

**Mechanical properties:**
- The pack element is semi-controlled: it responds to broad commands (attack, retreat, scatter, consolidate) but not precise orders
- Forested terrain within range of The First Wild grows — unforested terrain near them begins generating light forest cover over time (mechanically: revealing the forest mechanics in terrain that previously didn't have them)
- All Wild units in range gain maximum possible faction bonuses
- Enemy units approaching The First Wild through forest are revealed to them (perfect vision in forest for The First Wild's range)
**Weakness:** In open terrain, The First Wild is extremely powerful but not supernatural — the terrain bonus is lost, the pack responds less cohesively
**Creation requirement:** Grand Sanctuary, sustained maximum Wild faith intensity, Level 5 progression, significant wild territory under control

---

## Elite Units

Elite warriors and commanders may arise through battlefield achievements and dynastic training systems. Exceptional commanders and war heroes can elevate the prestige of the dynasty and establish lesser houses.

Bloodline commanders can influence battlefield morale (future design idea).

## Born of Sacrifice Units

Born of Sacrifice is a late-game elite army-forging mechanic. Players may sacrifice multiple armies to create a powerful elite force. Example: five standard armies sacrificed to produce one elite force.

**Elite Army Benefits:**
- Siege capability
- Enhanced armor
- Extreme morale

**Naming:** Units can be named by the player or assigned names through a random generator.

These units are rare, extremely powerful, and often become legendary figures within a match. The mechanic creates identity-rich, memorable battlefield units tied to the ideological and dynastic character of the civilization.

## Army Composition Rules

### Level 1 Typical Composition
- Militia providing numbers
- Swordsmen providing mobility and scouting
- Spearmen holding defensive formations
- Hunters or Bowmen providing ranged support

### General Army Requirements
Armies are composed of units drawn from the population and trained through military infrastructure. Armies require:
- Food supply
- Gold payment
- Leadership (bloodline commanders or appointed generals)
- Morale support

Leadership provides battlefield advantages.

### Recruitment System
Army recruitment is not chosen as a single doctrine but controlled through adjustable sliders. Dynasties can mix recruitment approaches including family obligations, paid soldiers, and faith volunteers.

Including daughters in the army may produce a negative attack buffer but a positive healing and sustainment buffer. This creates a meaningful strategic tradeoff.

### Military Buildings
- **Barracks** — Trains infantry
- **Training Grounds** — Improves soldier training
- **Armory** — Provides equipment upgrades
- **Fortified Keep** — Major defensive stronghold
- **Watchtower** — Provides early warning of enemy approach

---

*Units are added as they are designed.*

---

### Player Manual Unit Expansion (Added 2026-03-15)

## Troop Promotion System

Troops can be promoted after multiple successful battles. This creates a battlefield experience progression mechanic.

## Champion Emergence

Great champions can emerge from battle and continue as great generals or be granted land and form a lesser house under the banner.

## Born of Sacrifice — Specific Creation Concept

Sacrificing five armies with the right faith knowledge level creates an advanced-level army with heavy or siege-adjacent capability.

## Sustainment and Support Dynamics

Combat must include sustainment and support dynamics. A reckless war machine that lets soldiers die with little support burns through manpower quickly and can drive morale low. Morale, loyalty, and sustainment are core factors, not superficial bonuses. Dynasties that neglect care for wounded, logistics, and support experience internal and frontline consequences.

## Bloodline Commander Presence

When a bloodline member leads an army, the army gains dynastic weight — not merely a combat buff. Their presence affects morale, legitimacy, reputation, succession security, and the political meaning of the war itself.

======================================================================
# PART I — MATCH FLOW
======================================================================


--- MATCH_STRUCTURE ---

# Bloodlines — Match Structure

How a match begins, progresses, escalates, and resolves.

### Content Added 2026-03-15

## Match Phases

Bloodlines matches progress through four strategic stages. Two naming conventions exist in the archive (Level and Stage terminology). Both are preserved.

### Stage 1 — Founding (Level 1)
The player establishes their initial settlement, gathers resources, and begins population growth. At the end of this stage the player selects one of the four ancient faiths. The dynasty encounters tribes, ruins, and sacred sites during exploration.

### Stage 2 — Consolidation (Level 2)
The kingdom expands infrastructure and military capability while stabilizing territory. Building construction, population growth, and territorial loyalty become primary concerns.

### Stage 3 — Ideological Expansion (Level 3)
Faith systems begin influencing society and shaping political identity. Ideology increasingly influences society, conflict, and civilization identity. Dynastic politics and religious development intensify.

### Stage 4 — Irreversible Divergence (Level 4)
The civilization reaches its ideological apex and unlocks powerful late-game structures. This is the major irreversible divergence point (point of no return). Dynasties commit to strategic directions such as faith dominance or military supremacy.

**Level 5 — Apex (beyond the four stages):**
Grand endgame apex structure tier. Apex capabilities. Level 5 represents the culmination of the civilization's development.

## Victory Conditions

**Military Conquest** — Conquest with instability risk. Reckless warfare lowers morale, causes mutinies and defections. Military conquest is not simply "destroy everything."

**Economic / Currency Dominance** — Dynasty introduces own currency into the global economy. Economic victory occurs when the world economy becomes dependent on that dynasty's currency.

**Faith Victory / Divine Right** — Faith-based victory path through religious influence and dominance.

**Territorial Governance Attraction** — Smaller territories choose to join the dynasty willingly due to prosperity and infrastructure. Victory through attraction rather than conquest.

**Dynastic Prestige** — Prestige as victory path with bloodline members on battlefield in prestige dispute wars.

**Note:** Population loyalty is not favored as a standalone victory path.

## Pacing and Escalation

Early game focuses on expansion and survival. Mid game introduces dynastic politics and religious development. Late game introduces ideological wars and victory escalation.

Matches represent civilizational arcs, not just military escalation. A match is not just bigger armies over time. It is the evolution of a civilization's identity, faith, governance, and dynasty.

Late game events may include global coalitions against dominant dynasties via the Trueborn coalition response system.

## Short Match vs Long Match

Optional long matches run from 2 to 10+ hours. Large matches may extend well beyond ten hours in epic campaigns.

Long match mode supports extended strategic campaigns emphasizing territory development, dynastic expansion, faith influence, and long-term strategic planning.

The game's pacing, economy, dynasty systems, ideology systems, and population systems are meant to sustain long-term meaning and not peak too early.

## Multiplayer Considerations

Up to 10 players with AI kingdoms and minor tribes. Different strategic playstyles remain viable. The world functions as a living political landscape.

**Trueborn Coalition Response System:**
If the Trueborn city remains unconquered, it can intervene when one dynasty approaches victory too quickly. The city raises the Trueborn banner and recruits dynasties into a coalition against the emerging hegemon. Contribution history to the Trueborn city matters.

Detailed network rules, lobby systems, team structures, diplomacy toggles, and multiplayer matchmaking rules have not yet been fully defined.

---

*Match structure is refined as design sessions inform pacing decisions.*

---

### Player Manual Match Flow Expansion (Added 2026-03-15)

## Match Scale and Duration

Design target assumes extreme stress test games lasting six to ten hours that still feel coherent, balanced, and fair in the Command and Conquer sense. Map scale is roughly ten times the scale of typical RTS maps. Matches can be as short as two hours but are intentionally built for long sessions.

## Ten-Hour Match Rhythm

1. **Opening phase:** Securing survival, reading the world, laying foundations
2. **Early expansion phase:** First real commitments to economy, bloodline structure, scouting-led strategic identity
3. **Covenant decision phase:** Transforms exploration into spiritual direction
4. **Developing mid-game:** Reveals whether realm is structurally real or theatrically fragile
5. **Pre-specialization phase:** Multiple futures still possible but not indefinitely
6. **Level 4 divergence phase:** Broad contest becomes struggle among increasingly defined powers
7. **Late game:** Currency wars, divine claims, prestige crises, territorial attraction, demonic or holy manifestations, major hostage politics, Trueborn coalition response

The game changes genre texture as it progresses: begins as a severe large-scale RTS and gradually becomes something closer to a dynastic civilizational struggle.

## Early Game Philosophy

The early game establishes the identity of the dynasty, stability of the realm, tempo of expansion, and conditions under which later specialization choices will flourish or break down.

First phase simultaneous jobs:
1. Secure food and water (before almost everything else)
2. Establish housing at pace matching ambitions
3. Scout the world (not optional — faith choice depends on it)
4. Begin forming internal dynastic plan

Level 1 is not merely the first rung of a tech ladder. It is the phase in which the dynasty is still becoming itself in relation to the world.

## Mid-Game Transition

Begins when dynasty stops merely surviving and starts revealing its direction. Early decisions are tested for structural soundness. Bloodline management becomes visibly important. Player must stop thinking like conventional RTS player and start thinking like a ruler.

## Victory Condition Late-Game Escalation

Each victory condition has specific late-game escalation mechanics that reshape the world and serve as a climax. The game pushes toward late-game ideological escalation rather than simple bigger armies.

- **Economic:** Currency adoption forcing wars, timer for resolution
- **Faith:** Divine bloodline claim accepted by the world
- **Territorial:** Voluntary integration through governance attraction
- **Prestige:** Grand wars requiring 5-10 bloodline members per side to the death
- **Military:** Stability requirement, mutiny/defection risk for reckless conquest

## End-of-Match Rewards

XP earned even for non-winning placements. Being second, third, or fourth remains rewarding. XP contributes to progression system unlocking starting advantages, variations, cosmetics, and early bonuses for replayability.

## Recovery as Gameplay

Recovery from severe defeats is a real part of gameplay. Dynasties can bend without immediately breaking. After defeat: diagnose, stabilize (water, food, population, housing, morale, recruitment, faith, family tree), then consider revenge. A player who learns recovery will outperform a player who only knows how to snowball.

---

### Full Match Structure Expansion — Sessions 6-9 (Added 2026-03-19)

## The Sovereignty Seat — What Every Victory Path Is Pursuing

Every victory condition in Bloodlines is a different route to the same destination: recognition as the supreme authority over all lands. Not the largest dynasty. Not the most powerful military. The primary ruler — the bloodline (or agreed coalition with an agreed head) recognized by the world as the legitimate, supreme governing authority.

No bloodline wins by being second. No bloodline wins by being the most respected or the most feared without the world's recognition of ultimate authority. The sovereignty seat is the Iron Throne equivalent in mechanical terms — it represents the answer to the question every match is asking: which bloodline, or coalition with an agreed head, the world of this match will recognize as its ruler.

This framing governs why every victory path is extremely difficult. It is not difficult because the design wants to punish players. It is difficult because legitimate supreme authority is rare in the real world, and the systems are designed to reflect that reality. The game naturally trends toward stalemate, coalition formation, and political resolution rather than clean solo conquest. A match that ends in a single bloodline's absolute sovereignty is remarkable. A match that ends in an Alliance Victory or a Divine Right claim is equally remarkable. Both are correct endings.

## Six Victory Paths — Expanded

### 1. Military Conquest

The most direct path. Destroy enemy armies, capture enemy territory, eliminate enemy governance. When enough territory has been held with sufficient stability — not just captured, but governed — the conquest qualifies as completion.

The critical mechanic: reckless conquest causes Legitimacy Erosion. Territory held by force without governance infrastructure generates loyalty collapse events. Armies that fight without moral conviction begin experiencing attrition, mutiny, and defection. A dynasty that has captured 70% of the map but is governing it at 40% stability is not approaching Military Conquest — it is approaching internal collapse.

The path requires: sustained military capacity, governance development pacing military acquisition, sufficient conviction to prevent army deterioration, and enough diplomatic management to prevent coalition formation.

**Late-game escalation:** The Great Reckoning. When any dynasty holds 70%+ of map territory, all remaining dynasties receive a coalition bonus and the Trueborn mobilizes. The Reckoning is the structural immune response of the match to imminent military domination.

### 2. Economic Dominance — Currency Standard

Full specification in RESOURCE_SYSTEM.md. The dynasty replaces gold with its own currency as the world's economic standard, achieving the capacity to purchase loyalty and starve rival economies.

**Late-game escalation:** Resolution Battle trigger. When currency dominance threshold approaches, dynasties that refuse adoption may demand a military confrontation to resolve the question of whose economic standard governs the world.

### 3. Faith Divine Right

The dynasty formally declares that its bloodline descends from the covenant's highest divine power and has the right to rule all lands. The declaration initiates a spread window during which the claim must propagate through the world's faith population.

Full specification in FAITH_SYSTEM.md. The declaration is public from the moment it is made. All milestones are visible before and after declaration. The drama is the race, not the surprise.

**Late-game escalation:** Coalition formation against the declaring dynasty. Every dynasty whose faith covenant is different — and some whose faith is the same — have reasons to prevent one bloodline from claiming divine sovereignty.

### 4. Territorial Governance

Every province under the dynasty's control — including all tribal territories — must reach loyalty above 80%, infrastructure at settlement tier or higher, and active economic production. The population must have had time to develop organic attachment to the dynasty's rule.

This path requires the consent of the governed. It is not military occupation. It is development. Revolts are constant. Newly acquired territories resist. Tribal lands are the hardest — their populations have managed their own affairs for decades and do not transfer loyalty easily.

**Late-game escalation:** Voluntary integration events. At a threshold of development excellence, smaller territories begin approaching the dynasty unprovoked, seeking prosperity. This is both confirmation that the path is working and acceleration toward the victory threshold.

### 5. Dynastic Prestige

The dynasty accumulates sufficient prestige through bloodline member performance on the battlefield in formal prestige disputes. Grand Prestige Wars require approximately five to ten bloodline members per side on the battlefield, with outcomes determined by their performance and deaths. The prestige accumulated through these deaths — and the recognition of what was sacrificed — constitutes the dynasty's claim to supreme authority.

**Late-game escalation:** Grand Prestige Wars become the primary match conflict at late stage for dynasties pursuing this path. The death of a Legendary Bloodline Member in a Prestige War is a match-announced event with diplomatic and conviction consequences beyond the immediate prestige outcome.

### 6. Alliance Victory (Added — Seventh Ingestion)

Two or more bloodlines formally agree on terms of shared rulership with one agreed-upon ultimate ruler. The sovereign holder's identity, the coalition partners' obligations, and the territory arrangements must be ratified before the victory mechanism activates.

Once terms are ratified, sixty-five percent of the world's total population must come to accept the coalition's authority. This is not automatic. Active governance work is required: faith infrastructure deployment, economic investment, military presence. Revolt continues throughout the acceptance clock.

The internal dynamics of maintaining the coalition are a political game within the political game. The dynasty holding the sovereignty seat has the strongest incentive for the victory clock to proceed. Coalition partners may calculate that using the coalition to reach dominance and then defecting is more advantageous than completing the Alliance Victory. Managing this pressure while simultaneously managing the acceptance clock is the core challenge.

**Difficulty standard:** Alliance Victory is VERY VERY VERY difficult — the same standard as every other path. It is not a consolation path. It is a complex coordination problem under adversarial conditions.

## The Trueborn Rise Arc — Match Timing

The Trueborn City is not passive throughout the match. If no dynasty has claimed the city by the activation threshold — a point in match time calculated from match length and current territorial development — the Trueborn raise their banners and become an active NPC faction pursuing their own reconquest.

**Before activation threshold:** The Trueborn City functions as the neutral trade hub, information network, and diplomatic guarantee holder described in the lore. All dynasties have interest in maintaining access to the city.

**At activation threshold (if unclaimed):** The Trueborn rise arc activates. The Trueborn house declares their intent to reassert the ancient authority they held before The Great Frost. They begin actively expanding territory, seeking alliances with dynasties that have contributed to the city's stability, and opposing dynasties that have not.

**Why this matters:** A dynasty that has been ignoring the Trueborn City suddenly faces a powerful, historically legitimate rival with institutional gravity that no bloodline can simply dismiss. The Trueborn rise arc creates a forced pressure mechanic — the city cannot be ignored indefinitely, and the cost of ignoring it is facing the city as an adversary with the legitimacy advantage.

**Conquering the city:** Holding the Trueborn City is not a victory condition. It is a strategic objective that provides significant bonuses to whatever victory path the conquering dynasty is pursuing. The information network, trade amplification, and diplomatic influence of a conquered city are substantial. But conquest does not end the match — it accelerates the path toward ending it.

## AI Kingdoms and Minor Tribes

**AI Kingdoms:** Matches at 1-10 players can include AI-controlled dynasties that fill the remaining founding house slots. AI kingdoms pursue viable victory paths, respond to diplomatic overtures, form coalitions, and create the political landscape pressure that keeps the match interesting even at low player counts. AI kingdoms are not targets to farm — they are participants in the match's politics with genuine strategic goals.

**Minor Tribes:** Ten archetype types distributed across the map. Minor tribes are not founding houses but they are not passive either. They have territory, military capability, and diplomatic responses. A dynasty that mistreats nearby tribes faces armed resistance. A dynasty that invests in tribal relationships can form alliances that provide military support, resource access, or buffer zones against rival dynasties. The Trueborn house is classified as a special tribe (not a founding house) until the rise arc activates or they are selected as a player bloodline.

**Minor tribe conquest outcomes:** Four options — assimilation (population joins dynasty, territory integrated), tributary arrangement (tribe remains semi-autonomous but pays tribute), alliance (tribe maintains autonomy, provides military support when called), conquest and dissolution (tribe is eliminated, surviving population absorbed or exiled). Each outcome has conviction effects depending on how it is handled.

## Stage 1 — Founding: Detailed Match Design

Stage 1 is the period in which the dynasty is still becoming itself in relation to the world. Every resource decision in Stage 1 constrains Stage 2. Every bloodline member commitment made in Stage 1 shapes the dynasty's identity for the rest of the match.

**Priority sequencing:** Food and water before everything. Housing before military. Scouting before faith commitment. The player who skips any of these in favor of aggressive military expansion in Stage 1 is building an army on an unstable foundation.

**Faith selection timing:** At the end of Stage 1, the dynasty selects one of the four ancient faiths. This selection is informed by the scouting that happened during Stage 1 — what terrain is accessible (Sacred Ground nearby favors faith investment), what rival dynasties appear to be doing (a rival committing heavily to Blood Dominion early changes the calculus for committing to Old Light), and what victory path the player is gravitating toward.

**Starting leader significance:** The founding bloodline member who leads Stage 1 sets the dynasty's initial conviction trajectory. Their presence or absence in combat, their governance decisions, and their interactions with early events all generate conviction points before the player has full system literacy. Stage 1 ends with the dynasty having already begun to acquire a conviction character.

## Stage 2 — Consolidation: Detailed Match Design

Stage 2 is where structural soundness reveals itself.
